properties.name: The Elephant House
properties.description: The coffee house where J.K. Rowling sat writing the early Harry Potter books.
properties.fun_fact: Rowling wrote here with a view of Edinburgh Castle. The back room became a place of pilgrimage for fans.

properties.name: Victoria Street
properties.description: The curved street with colourful shopfronts widely said to have inspired Diagon Alley.
properties.fun_fact: Look closely at the shop signs – several lean happily into the wizarding connection.

properties.name: Greyfriars Kirkyard
properties.description: The kirkyard where the gravestone of Thomas Riddell is believed to have inspired Tom Riddle – Lord Voldemort.
properties.fun_fact: Also buried here: William McGonagall, famously the worst poet in Britain – and quite possibly a certain Professor’s namesake.

properties.name: George Heriot's School
properties.description: The grand 17th-century school said to have inspired Hogwarts.
properties.fun_fact: The school sorts its pupils into four houses – sound familiar?

properties.name: The Balmoral Hotel
properties.description: Where J.K. Rowling finished Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows in 2007. The room is now the J.K. Rowling Suite.
properties.fun_fact: She signed a marble bust in the room: "J.K. Rowling finished writing Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows in this room (552) on 11th Jan 2007".

properties.name: Glenfinnan Viaduct
properties.description: The iconic railway viaduct the Hogwarts Express crosses in the films. Loch Shiel below also appears as the Black Lake.
properties.fun_fact: The flying Ford Anglia chases the train here in Chamber of Secrets – with Harry hanging out of the door.

properties.name: The Jacobite – the Hogwarts Express route
properties.description: The closest you can get to riding the Hogwarts Express: a real steam train running Fort William–Mallaig across the Glenfinnan Viaduct.
properties.fun_fact: This is the actual locomotive type used as the Hogwarts Express in the films.

properties.name: Glencoe
properties.description: Dramatic Highland scenery used for exteriors in Prisoner of Azkaban and Goblet of Fire.
properties.fun_fact: Hagrid’s hut was built here for Prisoner of Azkaban – near the Clachaig Inn.

properties.name: Glen Nevis and Steall Falls
properties.description: The glen at the foot of Britain’s highest mountain hosted Hogwarts grounds scenes; Steall Falls appears in the Triwizard Tournament.
properties.fun_fact: Quidditch scenes in the early films were set against this landscape.

properties.name: Goathland Station
properties.description: The North Yorkshire Moors Railway station that became Hogsmeade Station in Philosopher’s Stone.
properties.fun_fact: Built in 1865 and barely changed since – no set dressing needed.

properties.name: Malham Cove
properties.description: The vast limestone cliff in the Yorkshire Dales where Harry and Hermione camp in Deathly Hallows: Part 1.
properties.fun_fact: They pitched the tent on the limestone pavement at the very top – worth the climb.

properties.name: Alnwick Castle
properties.description: Hogwarts exterior in the first two films – including Madam Hooch’s first flying lesson.
properties.fun_fact: "Up!" – the broomstick lesson happened on the lawns here. The Ford Anglia also crash-landed here in Chamber of Secrets.

properties.name: Gloucester Cathedral
properties.description: The medieval cloisters became the Hogwarts corridors in Philosopher’s Stone, Chamber of Secrets and Half-Blood Prince.
properties.fun_fact: "The Chamber of Secrets has been opened…" – the writing on the wall scene was shot in these cloisters.

properties.name: Lacock Abbey
properties.description: Used across four films: Snape’s potions classroom, Quirrell’s classroom and Hogwarts corridors.
properties.fun_fact: The Mirror of Erised stood here, in the Chapter House.

properties.name: Lacock Village
properties.description: The medieval village that played Budleigh Babberton in Half-Blood Prince.
properties.fun_fact: Horace Slughorn hid here – disguised as an armchair.

properties.name: Hardwick Hall
properties.description: The Elizabethan mansion that became Malfoy Manor in Deathly Hallows.
properties.fun_fact: "More window than wall", they said in 1597 – digitally sharpened into the Malfoys’ forbidding home.

properties.name: Lavenham
properties.description: The crooked timber-framed village that became Godric’s Hollow in Deathly Hallows: Part 1.
properties.fun_fact: The De Vere House stood in for the Potters’ cottage – where it all began.

properties.name: Christ Church, Oxford
properties.description: The staircase where Professor McGonagall greets the first-years in Philosopher’s Stone. The Great Hall inspired Hogwarts’ own.
properties.fun_fact: Hogwarts’ Great Hall was built in the studio – but modelled on the dining hall here.

properties.name: Bodleian Library, Oxford
properties.description: Duke Humfrey’s Library became the Hogwarts library; the Divinity School became the hospital wing.
properties.fun_fact: Harry sneaks through here under the Invisibility Cloak in the Restricted Section scene.

properties.name: New College, Oxford
properties.description: The medieval cloister and courtyard where Draco Malfoy gets turned into a ferret in Goblet of Fire.
properties.fun_fact: "Mad-Eye" Moody’s amazing bouncing ferret scene happened under the great oak here.

properties.name: Durham Cathedral
properties.description: The Norman cathedral’s cloisters became Hogwarts courtyards and classrooms in the early films.
properties.fun_fact: Harry releases Hedwig into the snow here – and McGonagall’s Transfiguration class met in the Chapter House.

properties.name: Warner Bros. Studio Tour London
properties.description: The Making of Harry Potter – the original sets, props and costumes from all eight films.
properties.fun_fact: The Great Hall, Diagon Alley and the original Hogwarts Express – plus Butterbeer.

properties.name: King’s Cross – Platform 9¾
properties.description: Where the Hogwarts Express departs every year on 1 September. The luggage trolley in the wall is now a landmark.
properties.fun_fact: The film scenes were actually shot between platforms 4 and 5 – the station’s own secret.

properties.name: Leadenhall Market
properties.description: The Victorian covered market used as the way to the Leaky Cauldron and Diagon Alley in Philosopher’s Stone.
properties.fun_fact: The blue door in Bull’s Head Passage was the entrance to the Leaky Cauldron.

properties.name: Australia House
properties.description: The exterior of Gringotts Wizarding Bank. The marble Exhibition Hall inspired the bank’s interior.
properties.fun_fact: The goblin-filled banking hall itself was built in the studio – modelled on the marble hall inside.

properties.name: Claremont Square
properties.description: Filmed as Number 12, Grimmauld Place – headquarters of the Order of the Phoenix and home of the Black family.
properties.fun_fact: The house that squeezes itself into view between the neighbours – no Muggles noticed a thing.

properties.name: Millennium Bridge
properties.description: Torn apart by Death Eaters in the opening of Half-Blood Prince.
properties.fun_fact: The bridge really did wobble when it opened in 2000 – Londoners nicknamed it the Wobbly Bridge.

properties.name: Reptile House, London Zoo
properties.description: Where Harry first speaks Parseltongue to a boa constrictor – and Dudley ends up behind the glass.
properties.fun_fact: "Thanksss, amigo." The snake was off to Brazil.

properties.name: Goodwin's Court
properties.description: A narrow 17th-century alley of bowed glass windows and gas lamps – a key inspiration for Diagon Alley and Knockturn Alley.
properties.fun_fact: Come at dusk when the gas lamps glow – it is the closest London gets to Knockturn Alley.

properties.name: Freshwater West, Pembrokeshire
properties.description: The remote Welsh beach where Shell Cottage stood – and where Dobby is buried in Deathly Hallows.
properties.fun_fact: "Here lies Dobby, a free elf." Fans still leave socks on the beach.

properties.name: Ministry of Magic entrance – Great Scotland Yard
properties.description: Scotland Place and Great Scotland Yard played the visitors’ entrance to the Ministry of Magic – red telephone box and all.
properties.fun_fact: Harry and Mr Weasley dialled 6-2-4-4-2 – MAGIC – in the phone box here in Order of the Phoenix.

properties.name: Forest of Dean
properties.description: The ancient forest where Harry and Hermione camp in Deathly Hallows – and where the silver doe leads Harry to the Sword of Gryffindor.
properties.fun_fact: Hermione’s parents took her here as a child – and J.K. Rowling grew up in Tutshill, on the forest’s edge.

properties.name: Charing Cross Road
properties.description: The canonical home of the Leaky Cauldron – the wizarding gateway between Muggle London and Diagon Alley.
properties.fun_fact: The Palace Theatre on this street has hosted Harry Potter and the Cursed Child since 2016.

properties.name: Westminster Underground Station
properties.description: Harry and Mr Weasley take the Underground here on the way to Harry’s hearing at the Ministry of Magic.
properties.fun_fact: The station was closed for a full day of filming – Mr Weasley remained delighted by the ticket barriers.

properties.name: Borough Market – Stoney Street
properties.description: The Leaky Cauldron’s exterior in Prisoner of Azkaban – the Knight Bus squeezes between Muggle traffic right here.
properties.fun_fact: The pub entrance was the flower shop at 7 Stoney Street; next door became the Third Hand Book Emporium.

properties.name: Piccadilly Circus
properties.description: Harry, Ron and Hermione flee through the crowds here after the Death Eaters crash the wedding.
properties.fun_fact: They narrowly miss a double-decker bus on Shaftesbury Avenue – Apparition in central London is precision work.

properties.name: Houses of Parliament
properties.description: The Order of the Phoenix flies Harry down the Thames past Parliament and Big Ben on the way to Grimmauld Place.
properties.fun_fact: The broomstick flight follows the real Thames route – you can trace it on foot along the Embankment.

properties.name: Eilean na Mòine, Loch Eilt
properties.description: The tiny islet that became Dumbledore’s final resting place – the white tomb was added digitally.
properties.fun_fact: You pass it on the Jacobite steam train and the A830 – bring the camera ready between Glenfinnan and Lochailort.

properties.name: Loch Shiel
properties.description: The loch below Glenfinnan stood in for the Black Lake – including the second task of the Triwizard Tournament.
properties.fun_fact: The Durmstrang ship rises from these waters in Goblet of Fire.

properties.name: Black Park
properties.description: The Forbidden Forest of the early films – dense pines conveniently next door to Pinewood Studios.
properties.fun_fact: Hagrid leads the first-years’ detention into these woods in Philosopher’s Stone.

properties.name: Picket Post Close, Martins Heron
properties.description: Number 4, Privet Drive in Philosopher’s Stone. NB: an ordinary residential street – please admire quietly from the pavement.
properties.fun_fact: Later films rebuilt Privet Drive at Leavesden – the studio set is on the Studio Tour.

properties.name: Paddington Station
properties.description: In the book, Hagrid and Harry wait here for the train back to the Dursleys – Hagrid knitting what looks like a canary-yellow circus tent.
properties.fun_fact: Hagrid buys Harry a hamburger here – magic and Muggle fast food, side by side.

properties.name: Tottenham Court Road
properties.description: In the book, the trio Apparate here after the wedding attack and duck into an all-night café – where two Death Eaters find them.
properties.fun_fact: Hermione had a knack for escapes: the beaded bag with everything they needed was already packed.

properties.name: House of MinaLima
properties.description: The gallery of MinaLima – the design duo behind the Marauder’s Map, the Daily Prophet and every letter from Hogwarts.
properties.fun_fact: Miraphora Mina and Eduardo Lima designed the graphic universe of all eight films – entry to the gallery is free.
